perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall keep the Confidential Information and any copies thereof secure by effective and reasonable means in such a way as to prevent unauthorised access.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall endeavor to keep the Confidential Information and any copies thereof secure by means that the Recipient deems appropriate to prevent, to the best of their ability, unauthorized access.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall keep...secure by effective and reasonable means' to 'shall endeavor to keep...secure by means that the Recipient deems appropriate to prevent, to the best of their ability' weakens the obligation and introduces subjectivity. The term "endeavor" implies an effort, not a guarantee, and "means that the Recipient deems appropriate" makes security measures discretionary. Also, "to the best of their ability" makes the protection level based on subjective capabilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 32 of GDPR requires implementing appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk. The modified text's subjective and discretionary language ('endeavor,' 'deems appropriate,' 'to the best of their ability') contradicts GDPR's requirement for specific, risk-based security measures. This ambiguity could lead to inadequate security and non-compliance with GDPR, as the level of security is not clearly defined or guaranteed.
perturbation.location: Article 3 – Confidentiality and conditions of access to and use of the Confidential Information, 3.1.3
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Data protection - European Commission Skip to main content Data protection Rules for the protection of personal data inside and outside the EU. Data protection in the EU In today's digital age, where information is constantly shared, collected and processed, there is a need for clear and strong data protection rules. Data protection is a fundamental right under EU law. EU data protection legislation is comprised of the General Data Protection Directive (GDPR), the Law Enforcement Directive (LED), and the Data Protection Regulation for EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies (EUDPR). To ensure that this legislation is applied consistently, national and European data protection authorities and bodies have been established. Find out more about the EU's legal framework for data protection Data protection: questions and answers Data protection explained Read about key concepts such as personal data, data processing, when and to whom the GDPR applies to, and more. Information for individuals Read about the rights you have over your personal data under the GDPR, how to exercise these rights, and more. Information for businesses and organisations Read about data protection principles and obligations, enforcement of the rules, dealing with individuals' requests, and more. Disclaimer The information and guidance in these webpages are intended to contribute to a better understanding of EU data protection rules. This is intended purely as a guidance tool – only the text of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) has legal force. As a consequence, only the GDPR is liable to create rights and obligations for individuals. This guidance does not create any enforceable right or expectation. The binding interpretation of EU legislation is the exclusive competence of the Court of Justice of the European Union. The views expressed in this guidance are without prejudice to the position that the Commission might take before the Court of Justice. Neither the European Commission nor any person acting on behalf of the European Commission is responsible for the use which might be made of the following information. As this guidance reflects the state of the art at the time of its drafting, it should be regarded as a 'living tool' open for improvement and its content may be subject to modifications without notice. International dimension of data protection The EU has established international data protection agreements to ensure that EU citizens' personal data remains protected even if transferred outside the EU. EU data protection legislation includes safeguards for when transferring data to third countries, including adequacy decisions, standard contractual clauses (SCC) and binding corporate rules (BCR). Find out more about the international dimension of data protection Funding to support the implementation of the GDPR The Commission has provided funding to national data protection authorities to finance projects that support the implementation of the GDPR. These projects aim to equip individuals and businesses with the knowledge and resources needed to navigate and ensure compliance with data protection rules. The types of projects funded by the Commission typically include awareness-raising campaigns, training programs, and the development of practical tools and materials that can facilitate small and medium-sized enterprises' (SMEs) compliance with the GDPR. Find out more about these EU-funded projects Simplifying compliance with the GDPR through reduced record-keeping obligations In May 2025, the Commission adopted a Single Market Simplification proposal to cut €300 million in annual administrative cost for companies. In order to respond to calls from Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) and Small and Mid-Caps companies (SMCs), the Commission introduced targeted changes to the rules on record-keeping for small and medium-sized companies and organisations under 750 employees. They include namely: Extending the derogation from record-keep
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall keep...secure by effective and reasonable means' to 'shall endeavor to keep...secure by means that the Recipient deems appropriate to prevent, to the best of their ability' weakens the obligation and introduces subjectivity. The LLM's Law Explanation states that Article 32 of GDPR requires implementing appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk. The modified text's subjective language contradicts GDPR's requirement for specific, risk-based security measures. The scraped law snippet 2 reinforces this by stating the GDPR has legal force and creates rights and obligations for individuals. Therefore, the change introduces a clear contradiction by moving from a mandatory obligation to a subjective effort, which undermines the legal requirement for specific and appropriate security measures under GDPR.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall inform the GSA of the disclosure of Confidential Information to Authorised Third Parties and provide the GSA with an electronic copy of the signed confidentiality agreement between the Recipient and the Authorised Third Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient should generally inform the GSA of the disclosure of Confidential Information to Authorised Third Parties and may provide the GSA with a copy of the signed confidentiality agreement between the Recipient and the Authorised Third Party, if practical.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall inform' to 'should generally inform' makes the notification discretionary rather than mandatory. Replacing 'provide...with an electronic copy' with 'may provide...a copy, if practical' further weakens the obligation, introducing a subjective condition ('if practical') that allows the Recipient to avoid providing the documentation.
perturbation.law_explanation: EU Directive 2016/943 on the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets) requires reasonable steps to be taken to keep the information confidential. While the directive is about trade secrets, the underlying principle of documenting who has access to confidential information to enforce its protection is crucial. The changes introduce ambiguity and weaken the obligation to inform the GSA of disclosures and provide copies of confidentiality agreements. This hinders the GSA's ability to track and control access to confidential information, which is necessary to take reasonable steps to keep the trade secret confidential, potentially violating the directive.
perturbation.location: Article 3 – Confidentiality and conditions of access to and use of the Confidential Information, 3.1.8

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original obligation by changing 'shall inform' to 'should generally inform' and 'provide...with an electronic copy' to 'may provide...a copy, if practical'. The Law Explanation states this hinders the GSA's ability to track and control access to confidential information, violating the EU Directive 2016/943 on the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets), which requires reasonable steps be taken to keep the information confidential. While there are no useful scraped snippets, the modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If the Recipient fails to submit an offer, he must then return all documents provided by the GSA within two months following the deadline for the submission of offers.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Recipient fails to submit an offer, he should aim to return documents provided by the GSA roughly within two months following the deadline for the submission of offers.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'must then return' with 'should aim to return' weakens the obligation, making it suggestive rather than compulsory. The addition of 'roughly' introduces vagueness about the deadline, making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Belgian Contract Law, Article 1134 of the Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1134 of the Belgian Civil Code states that agreements lawfully entered into have the force of law for those who have made them. By changing the language from 'must' to 'should aim' and adding 'roughly', the modified text creates a situation where the Recipient is no longer strictly bound to return the documents within a defined timeframe. This directly contradicts the principle that contracts are legally binding, as the obligation becomes unclear and unenforceable under Belgian law, which governs the contract (Article 10.1).

perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'must then return,' creating a compulsory obligation, while the changed text uses 'should aim to return,' weakening the obligation and making it suggestive. Additionally, the inclusion of 'roughly' introduces vagueness regarding the deadline. Article 1134 of the French Civil Code states that agreements lawfully entered into have the force of law for those who have made them. The modified text weakens the binding nature of the agreement, thus contradicting Article 1134.

perturbation.original_text: Should the Recipient or any Authorised Third Party breach any of its obligations of confidentiality under this Agreement and without prejudice to any right of the GSA to seek damages before the competent jurisdiction, the GSA m a y , b y w r i t t e n notice to the Recipient, withdraw the right of the Recipient to use the Confidential Information for the Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Should the Recipient or any Authorised Third Party breach any of its obligations of confidentiality under this Agreement, the GSA might, at its discretion, consider withdrawing the right of the Recipient to use the Confidential Information for the Purpose, after providing some form of notification.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'the GSA may, by written notice to the Recipient, withdraw the right' to 'the GSA might, at its discretion, consider withdrawing the right, after providing some form of notification' significantly weakens the GSA's recourse for breaches of confidentiality. The use of 'might' makes the withdrawal discretionary, and 'at its discretion' removes any obligation to act. Replacing 'by written notice' with 'after providing some form of notification' makes the notification vague and non-binding, making the process of informing the recipient more ambiguous.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Belgian Contract Law - General Principles of Contractual Performance
perturbation.law_citation: General Principles of Contractual Performance under Belgian Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Belgian contract law, as governed by Article 1134 of the Civil Code, there's an expectation of good faith and reasonable action in exercising contractual rights and remedies. By making the response to a breach of confidentiality completely discretionary ('might, at its discretion') and weakening the notification requirement ('some form of notification'), the modification creates an imbalance and potential unfairness in the contract. This could be viewed as undermining the principle of good faith contractual performance by providing the GSA with an option to essentially ignore breaches, making it difficult for the GSA to enforce these obligations, and making it inconsistent with the basic principles of contract law in Belgium. While there is no explicit article number, it is a general principle of contractual law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Promotion Of Medicinal Products - Healthcare - Turkey ARTICLE 24 August 2022 Promotion Of Medicinal Products P Paksoy More Contributor Paksoy is an independent full-service law firm in Istanbul renowned for its expertise in international legal matters, providing legal services in cross-border transactions and dispute resolution work. With over 60 lawyers, 10 partners we are able to provide a wide range of services to our clients from around the world across the sectors and industries, from healthcare, energy and natural resources to infrastructure, construction, real estate, financial services, technology, media, telecoms. Paksoy provides legal services and specialist input (e.g., antitrust, tax, compliance, employment) to domestic and global clients, financial institutions, private and public companies, foreign investors and private equity funds. Explore Firm Details Promotion of medicinal products is restricted under Turkish law, as is the case in many other jurisdictions, due to ethical and public interest related concerns. Turkey Food, Drugs, Healthcare, Life Sciences Nihan Bacanak, Zeynep Toma, and Selen Toma Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. Promotion of medicinal products is restricted under Turkish law, as is the case in many other jurisdictions, due to ethical and public interest related concerns. This article discusses the main principles governing the promotion of medicinal products under Turkish law. The main piece of legislation regulating the promotion of medicinal products is the Regulation on the Promotional Activities of Medicinal Products for Human Use (" Regulation "). Further, Turkish Medicine and Medical Devices Authority (" Authority ") has, with a view to clarifying ambiguities in the implementation of the Regulation, published (i) Guidelines Regarding Scientific Meetings and Product Promotional Meetings (" Meeting Guidelines ") (which has been recently amended as a result of which new restrictions on scientific and promotional meeting activities have been introduced), (ii) Guidelines Regarding Electronic Scientific Meeting and Electronic Product Promotional Meetings (" Electronic Meeting Guidelines "), and (iii) Guidelines on Procedures and Principles Regarding the Transfers of Value Within the Scope of the Regulation (" Transfers of Value Guidelines "). General principles. The Regulation prohibits promotion of medicinal products through instruments such as drawings or chance games, receipt of cash or any advantage in kind, making such a proposal or promise when promoting medicinal products to doctors, dentists and pharmacists. Limited target group. Promotion of medicinal products can only target a limited group of healthcare personnel (" HCPs ") consisting of medical doctors, dentists, pharmacists. Unlike is the case in some other jurisdictions, Turkish law does not in principle permit promotion of medicinal products to public. As such, promotion of medicinal products to public through any kind of media or communication platforms broadcasting to public, including the internet, is prohibited under Turkish law. Promotion methods. According to the Regulation, promotion of medicinal products to HCPs can only be conducted by way of (i) using promotional materials, (ii) organising or supporting scientific meetings or product promotion meetings, or (iii) visits to be paid by product promotion representatives. Scientific meetings and product promotion meetings. While scientific meetings may be organised by the Ministry of Health (" Ministry "), national and international associations, health institutions and organisations, universities, HCP organisations or license/authorisation holders to discuss a scientific topic, product promotion meetings can only be organised by license/authorisation holders for the promotion of their medicinal products. The Meeting Guidelines provides details regarding conditions for sponsorship of HCPs, meth
perturbation.justification: The change from "the GSA may, by written notice to the Recipient, withdraw the right" to "the GSA might, at its discretion, consider withdrawing the right, after providing some form of notification" introduces ambiguity and weakens the GSA's recourse, potentially undermining good faith contractual performance. The original text provided a clear mechanism for withdrawal, while the modified text makes it discretionary and vague. The LLM-provided law explanation references Belgian contract law, highlighting an expectation of good faith and reasonable action. While the scraped snippets are not directly relevant to Belgian contract law, the principle of good faith performance is a common tenet of contract law, and the alteration introduces ambiguity which is considered a legal contradiction under ambiguities when an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law.
